title: National environmental monitoring and local enforcement strategies

data_scope.pm25_resolution: ~1 km daily satellite remote sensing (TAP framework)
data_scope.accessible_vs_nonaccessible_differential: 0.57% greater PM2.5 reduction in monitor-accessible cells
data_scope.city_station_count: nationwide across Chinese cities; exact count not stated in abstract/data statement

core_conclusion: The nationwide rollout of air-quality-monitoring stations cut urban PM2.5 by 8.03% in Chinese cities, but reductions were 0.57% larger in cells technically accessible to a monitor than in non-accessible cells. Local governments enforce strategically around sensors (less industrial activity, land-use change near monitors), producing nontrivial urban spatial inequalities in housing prices and mortality-related welfare. Monitoring technology should be designed with environmental justice in mind.
why_it_matters: Exposes how the limited spatial reach of monitoring sensors lets local enforcers concentrate effort where they are watched, turning a transparency tool into a source of within-city inequity that is capitalized into housing prices and health; argues for justice-aware sensor network design.
why_nature: Generalizable governance mechanism (monitoring shapes strategic enforcement) at city scale; couples sensor geography to firm behavior, land use, housing and mortality; credible staggered quasi-experiment on a nationwide rollout with 1 km satellite PM2.5; sustainability-and-equity framing.
added_value_to_my_research: Continuous monitoring-accessibility surface via sensor-side 2SFCA/R2SFCA -> dose-response enforcement effect
Explainable ML / MGWR on city-level differential -> attribution map of strategic enforcement
Sensor siting as efficiency-equity facility-location optimization (transfer of EV-charging framework)
Mobility-based, age-structured exposure model -> finer welfare-inequality recomputation
Physics-informed graph model with advection/diffusion -> detect pollution leakage vs true abatement
